title: At Port Royal
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tent-lights glimmer on the land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ship-lights on the sea;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The night-wind smooths with drifting sand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our track on lone Tybee.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At last our grating keels outslide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our good boats forward swing;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And while we ride the land-locked tide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our negroes row and sing.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For dear the bondman holds his gifts
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of music and of song
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The gold that kindly Nature sifts
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Among his sands of wrong:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The power to make his toiling days
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And poor home-comforts please;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The quaint relief of mirth that plays
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With sorrowן¿½s minor keys.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Another glow than sunsetן¿½s fire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Has filled the west with light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where field and garner, barn and byre,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are blazing through the night.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The land is wild with fear and hate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The rout runs mad and fast;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From hand to hand, from gate to gate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The flaming brand is passed.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lurid glow falls strong across
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dark faces broad with smiles:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not theirs the terror, hate, and loss
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That fire yon blazing piles.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With oar-strokes timing to their song,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They weave in simple lays
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The pathos of remembered wrong,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hope of better days,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The triumph-note that Miriam sung,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The joy of uncaged birds:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Softening with Africן¿½s mellow tongue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their broken Saxon words.



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Song of the Negro Boatmen.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, praise anן¿½ tanks! De Lord he come
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To set de people free;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anן¿½ massa tink it day ob doom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anן¿½ we ob jubilee.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De Lord dat heap de Red Sea waves
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He jusן¿½ as ן¿½trong as den;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He say de word: we lasן¿½ night slaves;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To-day, de Lordן¿½s freemen.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De yam will grow, de cotton blow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll hab de rice anן¿½ corn:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh nebber you fear, if nebber you hear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De driver blow his horn!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ole massa on he trabbels gone;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He leaf de land behind:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De Lordן¿½s breff blow him furder on,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like corn-shuck in de wind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We own de hoe, we own de plough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We own de hands dat hold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We sell de pig, we sell de cow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But nebber chile be sold.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De yam will grow, de cotton blow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll hab de rice anן¿½ corn:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh nebber you fear, if nebber you hear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De driver blow his horn!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We pray de Lord: he gib us signs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dat some day we be free;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De norf-wind tell it to de pines,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De wild-duck to de sea;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We tink it when de church-bell ring,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We dream it in de dream;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De rice-bird mean it when he sing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De eagle when be scream.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De yam will grow, de cotton blow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll hab de rice anן¿½ corn:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh nebber you fear, if nebber you hear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De driver blow his horn!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We know de promise nebber fail,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anן¿½ nebber lie de word;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So like de ן¿½postles in de jail,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We waited for de Lord:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anן¿½ now he open ebery door,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anן¿½ trow away de key;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He tink we lub him so before,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We lub him better free.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De yam will grow, de cotton blow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll gib de rice anן¿½ corn:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh nebber you fear, if nebber you hear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De driver blow his horn!



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So sing our dusky gondoliers;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with a secret pain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And smiles that seem akin to tears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We hear the wild refrain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We dare not share the negroן¿½s trust,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor yet his hope deny;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We only know that God is just,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And every wrong shall die.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rude seems the song; each swarthy face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flame-lighted, ruder still:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We start to think that hapless race
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must shape our good or ill;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That laws of changeless justice bind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oppressor with oppressed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, close as sin and suffering joined,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We march to Fate abreast.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sing on, poor hearts! your chant shall be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our sign of blight or bloom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Vala-song of Liberty,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or death-rune of our doom!
meaning: About: John Greenleaf Whittier (December 17, 1807 ן¿½ September 7, 1892) was an American Quaker poet and forceful advocate of the abolition of slavery in the United States.
